actions.thought: In a 3-handed game, the button is the first to act pre-flop. Q7s is a playable suited connector, which holds good equity for opening from the button. I want to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand. A raise to 2.5x (250 chips) is a standard open size to build the pot and apply pressure on the blinds.
I have 93 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button raise. This is a very weak hand with extremely negative expected value if played, especially out of position. The optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 6d. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. It costs me 150 more to call into a total pot of 400. While I am getting decent pot odds (approx 2.6:1), Js 6d is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially in a 100BB deep-stack environment. Standard defending ranges against a Button open usually start around J7o or J8o. Given this is the first hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

KJo is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. Being on the button, I have position on the remaining players for the rest of the hand. Opening with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind is the optimal play to take the initiative and build the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 9c, a marginal hand. The Button has opened for 250. Calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand with a mediocre holding that is easily dominated. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but J9o is still on the bottom end of playability against a button raise. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for better spots.

I hold Ks Qc in the big blind, a strong starting hand that performs well post-flop. Facing a raise from the button, folding would be too passive given the pot odds and my hand strength. While 3-betting is an option to take the initiative, I prefer calling to control the pot size and evaluate the flop while out of position, as 250 is a reasonable price to see the flop.

I have missed the flop entirely with K-high on a board of Tc-3d-7h. Since I am first to act in the Big Blind, checking is the standard play. I lack the equity to bet as a bluff against the pre-flop aggressor and should look to keep the pot small or fold if the opponent makes a significant continuation bet.

The board is dry and disconnected (rainbow Tc 3d 7h), which is an excellent texture for a continuation bet. As the pre-flop raiser, I have a strong range advantage here. A small c-bet of around 1/3 of the pot should fold out a large portion of my opponent's defending range that completely missed, such as random overcards, Q-high, or suited connectors. Even if called, I still have K and J as overcards that can improve my hand on the turn.

The opponent has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 750 pot, giving me favorable pot odds to call. Holding King-high, I have two overcards which retain some equity against the opponent's range. Since the bet sizing is small, folding would be an overreaction, and raising is not advisable as I have no significant draw or made hand. Calling allows me to see the turn and re-evaluate.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with King-Queen high and a backdoor flush draw on a board of Tc-3d-7h-6c. Given that I have no made hand and limited equity, betting would be an unnecessary bluff. Checking is the standard play to let the pre-flop aggressor act, which allows me to see if they check back or make a bet I can evaluate against the pot odds.

The 6c on the turn does not improve our hand, and it completes some draws like 45 or gives 89 an open-ended straight draw while introducing a club flush draw. Our KJo has very little equity against hands that called the flop c-bet, such as any Ten, Seven, or pocket pairs. Firing a second barrel as a bluff here is unlikely to fold out those better hands. Checking behind is the best play to control the pot size, take a free card, and try to spike a King or Jack on the river.

I am holding King-high on a board of Tc-3d-7h-6c-Ad. The opponent checked the turn, which often indicates they have also missed or have a marginal showdown hand. Since I have no pair and very little chance of winning at showdown, checking is the most logical play. Betting would effectively be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any opponent holding even a low pair, and since there is no showdown value, checking is a standard approach to conclude the hand without risking more chips.

The Ace on the river is a perfect scare card. The opponent checked both the turn and the river, indicating they likely hold a marginal made hand like a Ten, a Seven, a medium pocket pair, or a missed draw. Since K-high has little showdown value against any pair they might have called with on the flop, turning our hand into a bluff to represent an Ace that just hit the river is a very profitable play. Betting around half pot should be sufficient to fold out those marginal pairs.

I hold King-high with no pair on a board of Tc-3d-7h-6c-Ad. The opponent checked the turn and is now betting the river after an Ace falls. Since I have no showdown value and cannot beat any hand that checks the turn and bets for value on the river, calling is mathematically incorrect. Even if the opponent is bluffing with lower cards, I cannot beat them. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I have Ace-Four of hearts on the button, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Suited Aces have excellent playability and nut-flush potential. Opening from the button allows me to exert pressure on the blinds and ensures I will have position for the rest of the hand if called. I will make a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot.

I hold a premium hand with Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind. Calling puts me out of position against the button opener, which is suboptimal. 3-betting to 1000 (roughly 3.3x the open) allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or define my range against the Button while keeping the pot manageable but building value. This is a standard aggressive play in this spot.

Facing an open raise from the Button and a large 3-bet from the Small Blind, 65o is far too weak to consider defending. It plays poorly out of position and is severely dominated by both players' ranges. The only correct action is to fold.

I opened with a standard raise to 300 on the button with Ace-Four suited, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight in the small blind has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While Ace-Four suited has good playability and nut-flush potential, calling a 3.3x 3-bet out of position (or in this case, even in position) can be risky early in the tournament without a solid read on the opponent's 3-betting range. A4s is often dominated by hands like AJ, AQ, or AK, and if an Ace hits the board, I could lose a significant portion of my stack. Given the early stage of the tournament, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips to a potentially dominated hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this position. Raising to 250 is a standard open that allows me to potentially take down the blinds or play the hand in position with the initiative.

I hold J2o in the Small Blind, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. The Button raised to 2.5x the big blind. Playing such a weak hand out of position is highly unprofitable, so the correct and easiest decision is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7h 8d. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a standard raise to 250. The Small Blind (Pro) folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, with a total pot size of 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.6:1. In a 3-handed game, a Button opening range is typically very wide, and 78o is a standard defending hand in this position given the price and the potential to flop equity. Although I will be out of position, the mathematical requirements for a call are met.

The flop is Tc-Kd-Kc, providing a paired board with high cards. As the Big Blind, I checked the flop after calling pre-flop. The button (Flashlight) was the pre-flop aggressor and their range contains more Kings and Tens than mine, but this board is quite static. Since it's my turn to act and the action has just reached me on the flop, I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. I have no piece of this board with 8-high and very little drawing potential, so I am looking to see a cheap turn or fold if faced with significant aggression.

The flop of Tc Kd Kc is a dry, paired board that interacts well with my opening range. Since my opponent checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play to maintain initiative, represent strength, and deny equity. While I only hold Ace-high, I have a gutshot straight draw and backdoor flush potential, providing enough equity to justify a bet of 250 into the 550 pot.

I have 8-high with no draws on a paired board of Tc-Kd-Kc. Flashlight was the pre-flop aggressor and is now continuation-betting half the pot. Since I have zero equity and am out of position, there is no reason to continue. Even with favorable pot odds of 4.2:1, calling with 8-high into a range that contains many Kings, Tens, and Ace-highs is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We are on the Button in a 3-handed game with Ace-Jack offsuit. This is a very strong starting hand to open the action. A standard raise to 2.5x (250) will likely steal the blinds or give us a strong positional advantage post-flop against worse hands.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. In 3-handed play, this is a premium hand against a button open. I will 3-bet to 850 to put pressure on the opener, discourage the Big Blind from calling, and take control of the pot. Given the stack depths, a 3.4x raise is a standard size out of position.

My hole cards are 6-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. I am in the Big Blind facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after a button raise. There is no justification for continuing with such a marginal hand out of position against a strong 3-bet range. Folding is the only disciplined option to preserve my stack.

We hold AJo on the button and are facing a large 3-bet from the Small Blind (from 250 to 850). While AJo is a good hand to open, it performs poorly against a standard 3-betting range which typically consists of big pairs and stronger broadways (AK, AQ). Calling here would leave us with reverse implied odds if we hit an Ace or Jack, and 4-bet bluffing is unnecessarily risky early in the tournament. Folding is the most prudent and mathematically sound decision.

I am on the button with Jh 4h. In a 3-handed game, the button opening range is typically wide, but J4s is near the bottom of that range and often a fold in many GTO strategies (usually J5s+ or J7s+ is preferred). Given that the opponents have shown some aggression in previous hands—specifically Flashlight 3-betting in Hand 3—I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to maintain a solid table image.

The button has folded, leaving me in the small blind against the big blind. Although 9-7 offsuit is a weak hand, this is a prime opportunity to steal the blinds with a wide opening range in a blind-versus-blind scenario. A raise to 250 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop if they fold, which is essential for maximizing stack size.
Facing a 2.5x raise from the small blind, our hand (86o) is simply too weak to defend. It is unsuited, poorly connected, and easily dominated. Folding is the clear and most +EV decision.

I am on the button with 7c Td. While this is a marginal hand, being in position provides a strong strategic advantage. I have been playing aggressively in previous hands, and opening the button with a raise to 250 is a standard move to pick up the blinds and maintain table control, even with a sub-optimal holding.
Q3o is a very weak hand to play from the small blind facing a button raise. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind holding T5o. Flashlight raised from the button to 250. While I'm getting decent odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 400), T5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an active player. Given it's early in the tournament (Hand 7 of 150), I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger range to contest pots.

A7o is a reasonable hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. I want to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot right here. I'll raise to a standard 2.5x to try and win the blinds uncontested, or at least play in position with an Ace blocker if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six suited (Jd 6d). The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x. While suited, J6 is a marginal hand to play out of position against an active opener. In a 3-handed game, I should be defending reasonably wide, but calling from the SB often invites the BB to squeeze or see a flop cheaply, leaving me sandwiched. Folding is a disciplined choice to avoid complex post-flop situations with a weak kicker.

Holding 8-3 offsuit is statistically one of the weakest starting hands. Facing a button raise while playing out of position, there is no viable equity to justify continuing. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve stack depth for better opportunities.
JTo on the Button is a standard raising hand in a 3-handed game. I'll open to 2.5x to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position.

Jack-five offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with from the small blind against a button open. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize equity, and calling here would put me in a disadvantaged spot throughout the hand. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
T8o is a weak offsuit gapper. Out of position against a Button raise, it's best to fold and conserve our stack.

My hand, 2h-3c, is arguably one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It lacks connectivity, high-card value, and suits. Even with position on the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips into this pot. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold immediately and preserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

Flashlight folded on the button, and action is on me in the small blind. I hold Q9o, which is a marginal offsuit hand. Playing this hand out of position against Flash, who has shown aggression in previous hands (like the 3-bet in Hand 5), is not ideal. Since Q9o is near the bottom of my opening range from the SB, the safest and highest EV decision is to simply fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with 7h 5d, which is an unsuited one-gapper and a very weak starting hand. Despite having the positional advantage, this hand lacks the equity and playability required to justify a pre-flop raise or call. The standard and most profitable long-term play here is to simply fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). In 3-handed play, any Ace is a strong starting hand when it's folded to the blinds. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, as Ace-Deuce plays better as the aggressor than by just completing the blind.

8c 4c is a very weak starting hand. While suited, it has low high-card value and does not play well out of position against an opponent who has shown aggression in previous hands. Calling 200 to see a flop with such a marginal holding is mathematically unfavorable and puts me in a difficult spot post-flop. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8h 9h, a strong speculative hand that plays very well in position. With blinds at 50/100 and no action before me, I will put in a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to exert positional pressure and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with almost no equity. Continuing here would be playing out of position against a pre-flop raiser. Folding is the standard and most profitable play to preserve my stack.
Q4o is a very weak starting hand, especially facing a 3x open raise from the Button. Folding is the only viable option to preserve chips.

K7 offsuit is a marginal hand that does not play well in multi-way pots, even with the button's positional advantage. Given that there is no raise in front of me, I could theoretically open, but K7o is weak enough that I prefer to wait for a better spot to enter the pot and conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5d, a very weak unsuited hand. The Button has folded, but against the Big Blind's range, playing an offsuit two-gapper out of position is a losing play. The optimal strategy here is to just fold and save my chips.
I am on the button with 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 93o, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to beat. While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands. Given the low quality of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand if called, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7d 2c, which is the mathematically weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, the hand is not worth a raise or even a call, especially since I am first to act in this three-handed rotation. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

My hand (3s, 5d) is extremely weak, lacking both high-card strength and potential for strong draws or made hands. Given the position and hand quality, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision to avoid unnecessary risk.

My hand, 3d 6d, is a low-ranked suited connector that lacks the strength to profitably open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game against two aggressive opponents. While button play is generally aggressive, I prefer to maintain a disciplined approach early in the tournament and wait for a hand with better equity to enter the pot.

Action folds to me in the small blind. King-Four suited is a strong enough hand to attack the big blind in a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising puts maximum pressure on Flash to defend with marginal holdings, and if called, we still have a suited King that can flop well.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am in position post-flop and the pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), 95o is a very weak hand that realizes its equity poorly. I have a healthy stack of nearly 100 BBs and don't need to defend with the bottom of my range this early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

26 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand. Since I am first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have no justification to open or call with this hand. The correct play is to simply fold and save my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 3s, which is a very weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While it is only 50 chips to call, 43o has very poor equity and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the strength of my hand, it is most profitable to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than defending or trying a low-probability bluff against Flashlight.

In a 3-handed game, J6s is on the periphery of a standard Button opening range. Given that the Small Blind (Flashlight) has been active and the Big Blind (Pro) is a competent player, I will choose to fold this marginal hand to maintain a disciplined image and avoid unnecessary risks early in the tournament with a weak kicker.

I am in the small blind position with 6h 7c. The dealer folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. While this is a weak hand, the pot odds and position justify completing the small blind to see a flop cheaply. I will call to complete my blind.
Out of position in the Big Blind with a weak offsuit hand (Th 5d) facing a SB limp. Checking my option is the most logical choice to see a free flop without inflating the pot.
I am holding 6-high on an Ace-high board with no pair or draw. My equity is extremely low, and there is no reason to bluff here into the Big Blind. Checking is the standard play to preserve my stack.

I have ten-high on an Ace-high board with no draws. My opponent checked, and taking a free turn card is the safest and most standard option. Betting here with zero equity and a weak hand as a pure bluff is unnecessary.

I am holding 7-high on a board of As-Qc-9h-3d. My hand has no showdown value and no meaningful equity. Since the Big Blind checked the flop and has a wide range that includes many Aces and Queens, any bluff attempt here is highly likely to be called. Checking is the only sensible play to minimize losses and potentially see a free river card.

Flashlight has checked twice, but my hand (Ten-high with no draw) has extremely poor equity. While a bluff might occasionally work, there is no pressing need to risk chips when taking a free river card is safe. I will check, keeping the pot small and evaluating the river.

The river has paired the Queen, and I hold 6-high with absolutely no showdown value. The Big Blind has checked through every street, signaling a very weak or capped range. Attempting a bluff here with 7-high is extremely unlikely to succeed against a range that could include small pairs or even total air, which would likely call or simply wait for my showdown. Checking behind is the most prudent play to minimize further loss, as I have no equity to justify a bet.

With Ten-high on an paired Ace-high board, I have essentially no showdown value. However, the pot is very small (200 chips) and my opponent has checked all three streets. While a bluff might fold out King-high or Jack-high, trying to steal a minimal pot here is an unnecessary risk. My opponent could easily have limped a hand with a 3, 9, or a weak Ace and would likely call a small bet. Checking behind and accepting the loss is the safest and lowest-variance play.

7c5s is an extremely weak hand with no playability or high-card strength. In a 3-handed game, opening or calling with this range is unprofitable. The best decision is to fold and wait for a better spot to enter a pot.

With A8 offsuit in the small blind after the button folds, I have a strong hand heads-up against the big blind. Raising to 300 (3x) puts pressure on the big blind to defend with marginal holdings, gives me the betting initiative, and will frequently pick up the pot right here.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket Deuces (2s 2d). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Given the previous hands, Pro appears to have a wide opening range from the small blind, having raised with hands like 95o previously. Pocket pairs have significant value heads-up, but 22 is vulnerable to overcards on the flop. Calling is the best option here to see a flop with a hand that has showdown value and set-mining potential without bloating the pot or over-committing to a 3-bet. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call.

With Ace-eight offsuit and no draws on a J-7-4 two-heart board, my hand has very little equity against a calling range. Being out of position, checking to evaluate the opponent's action is the safest approach. C-betting without any backdoor equity or strong draws on this texture will often lead to getting called or raised by better hands and draws. Checking allows me to control the pot size with my marginal Ace-high.

I have a small pocket pair on a board with two overcards (Jack and Seven). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked to me. While my deuces might be the best hand if Pro has missed with overcards like AK or Q10, they are very vulnerable. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, see a free turn card, and avoid being check-raised off my hand. This is a standard pot-control play with a marginal made hand in position.

With A8 offsuit on a 7-J-4-K board, we only have Ace high and no draws. The flop checked through, so our opponent's range could include marginal pairs, draws, or weak Broadway hands that just hit the King. Although the King favors my pre-flop raising range, betting here as a bluff is risky because our opponent's checking range on the flop contains many floats or traps. Checking allows us to see a free river and potentially realize our showdown value if the opponent also checks, or safely fold if the opponent bets.

The turn King is an overcard to my pocket deuces. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked both the flop and the turn, they are likely either weak or trapping with a marginal hand. While my deuces have very little showdown value, checking behind allows me to see the river for free and avoid being check-raised. I am content to pot control and see how the final card develops.

With Ace high and no pair on a 7-J-4-K-T board with three hearts, my hand has limited showdown value but could occasionally win against a completely busted draw or worse high card. Attempting a bluff here is risky, as our opponent's check-down range contains many marginal pairs (like 7x, 4x, or a newly paired Ten) that might hero-call a bet, and the river completes several straight and flush draws. Checking allows us to see a free showdown and is the most prudent play.

Pro has checked all three streets (flop, turn, and river) after raising pre-flop. This line typically indicates a marginal made hand or a complete miss that is looking for a cheap showdown. My pocket deuces are very weak on this board (7-J-4-K-T), as almost any hand in Pro's opening range (like Ace-high or small pairs) currently beats me. While I could attempt a bluff representing the flush or one of the overcards, the pot is relatively small (600 chips), and there is a risk Pro is planning to check-call with a hand like middle pair or an Ace. I will check behind to see a free showdown and conserve my stack.

Holding pocket Queens on the Button, this is a premium hand. I want to build the pot, extract value, and define the ranges of the blinds. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind is an optimal sizing to get action while maintaining control.

I am in the Small Blind with 97 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. 97o is a weak hand to play out of position, and calling here would likely lead to a difficult post-flop scenario. Given the stack sizes and the hand's equity against a standard button opening range, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

I am in the big blind with 8s-4c, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Even though the pot odds are tempting, calling out of position against a button raise with such a dominated hand is not a profitable long-term strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8c 5c, which is a marginal hand even in a 3-handed game. While opening from the button is generally a strong play, 85-suited is at the bottom of the range and can lead to difficult post-flop situations against active opponents like Flashlight and Pro. Given the current stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament (hand 21 of 150), I will choose to fold and wait for a stronger opening hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h-2c, which is a very weak hand. Even in a blind-vs-blind situation, it is mathematically inferior to play this hand. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

K9o is a reasonable hand to open from the button in a three-handed game. I want to apply pressure on the blinds to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play in position with the initiative. A raise to 250 is a standard opening size in this spot.

We have A9o in the small blind against a button open. A9o is generally too weak to call profitably out of position, and while it could be used as a 3-bet bluff occasionally, folding is the standard, lowest-variance play early in a tournament. We avoid playing a marginal offsuit Ace out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 5d. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. While I have decent pot odds (150 to call into a 400 pot, requiring ~27% equity) and my hand is suited, 35s is at the very bottom of any reasonable defending range. Playing this hand out of position against an active button will be difficult, and the hand lacks high-card strength. Given the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than bleed chips with marginal hands out of position.
I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with this holding. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. Since the Button has folded, it is a Blind vs Blind situation against Flashlight. A4o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in this spot to take down the blinds or play a pot with the initiative. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open.

Facing a 3x raise from the small blind while holding a weak hand (J2s) out of position. The hand has poor equity and playability post-flop against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with QTo, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Standard strategy dictates opening for a raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds. I will raise to 300 chips.

I am in the small blind holding suited connectors (Jack-Eight of hearts). While this hand has some playability, I am out of position against the button, who has shown aggression throughout the session. Given the stack depth and the disadvantage of being out of position, playing this hand is suboptimal. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot to enter the pot.

Holding pocket Queens (Qc Qd) in the Big Blind against a Button open is a premium hand and a mandatory 3-bet for value. The Button raised to 300. Out of position, I should size my 3-bet a bit larger to negate positional disadvantage and build the pot. A raise to 1000 is a standard and strong size here.

I opened with QTo from the button, which is a standard play in a 3-handed game. However, the Big Blind (Pro) has responded with a significant 3-bet to 1000. QTo is a hand that is frequently dominated by a 3-betting range (hands like AQ, KQ, or AT). While I would have position post-flop, the size of the raise and the potential for reverse implied odds (hitting a pair and losing to a better kicker) make this a risky spot to continue. At this stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity rather than calling a large raise with a marginal hand.

I am holding Queen-Three offsuit in the button position. This is a very weak hand that lacks potential for showdown value or significant equity against two opponents. Given that I have already folded in previous hands with similar strength, I will continue to play tight and fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind with T8o. T8 offsuit is a weak hand to play from out of position against the big blind. The button has already folded, but a raise here as a steal attempt with such weak equity isn't very profitable long-term, and calling (limping) leads to playing a marginal hand out of position. The most standard and conservative play is to fold and wait for better spots.
K3s is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal from the button. Opening to 2.5x (250) is my standard sizing from this position to put pressure on the blinds while keeping the risk relatively low.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four offsuit. The Button has opened with a 2.5x raise. While King-high has some raw equity, King-Four offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Defending this hand would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am often dominated or forced to fold to continued aggression. Given the stack depths and the fact that I have the Big Blind yet to act behind me, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind with 5c 6c, which is a solid suited connector. Getting 150 to call into a pot of 400 offers me good pot odds to see a flop. Calling is standard here to defend my blind, as my hand has decent playability and equity against a button open, and I will be in position to evaluate the flop.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Five offsuit. In this format, I need to be active from the button to capitalize on my positional advantage. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing I have used previously to steal the blinds and maintain the initiative.

Holding pocket Queens (QQ) in the Small Blind facing a 3x open from the Button. This is a premium hand and a mandatory 3-bet for value, to build the pot, and to isolate the raiser. I am in the Big Blind holding Jh 5c, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Flashlight) opened with a raise to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 1000. This indicates significant strength from Pro, and Jh 5c is nowhere near strong enough to call or 4-bet in this situation. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a marginal holding that is easily dominated by hands that call or 3-bet from the blinds. Given the deep stacks and 3-handed play, I prefer to fold these weaker offsuit hands and wait for better opportunities to open or steal.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. With 3d 6d in the Big Blind and the Small Blind limping, I have the option to check and see a free flop in position. My hand is a weak suited two-gapper, so there's no need to inflate the pot by raising. Taking a free flop is the best and standard play here.

The board is extremely low and dry ([3s, 2d, 2s]), and my hand ([9c, 6c]) is complete air with no pair or significant draw. As the Small Blind, I am out of position. The board is extremely dry and disconnected (3s-2d-2s). My hand (9c-6c) has no pair, no draw, and essentially zero equity against any reasonable continuation range from the opponent. With 3h9h on the button, I am looking at a weak suited hand that does not warrant an open-raise or a call. Even in a blind-stealing position, this hand is too weak to play profitably against two opponents. It is better to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

With J3 suited in the small blind, my hand is too weak to play profitably, even when it is folded to me. While attempting a steal is tempting, the poor post-flop playability of this hand if the Big Blind calls makes it a negative EV play. Folding is the safest and most optimal choice here.
52o is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. As the first to act on the Button, this is a clear and immediate fold.

I hold pocket Kings, which is a premium hand. With Flash raising from the small blind, the standard play is to 3-bet to build the pot and take control of the hand. I raised from the Small Blind with QJo, and the Big Blind (Flashlight) responded with a 3-bet to 900. In the Button position with Ten of Spades and Six of Diamonds, I have a relatively weak starting hand. With 9-10 offsuit in the small blind, I am out of position against the Big Blind. The hand is relatively weak, and while the pot odds (50 to win 100) are tempting, it is generally better to play more disciplined hands out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am holding 7-2 suited on the button. This is a very weak starting hand with little potential to connect with the board in a meaningful way. Even with position, playing this hand against two other players is negative expected value. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

J9o is a marginal hand in the small blind. While it can be played as a steal or a limp against the big blind, folding is a very reasonable and low-variance option, especially when playing out of position against an unknown calling range.

I am on the Button with 73o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given that this is a 3-handed game and I am first to act, there is no justification for entering the pot. Folding is the only sensible choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight of clubs (J8s). Since the Button has folded, it is a Blind vs. Blind situation. J8s is a solid hand in this spot, well above the folding range. I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (3c, 9s). Flash, acting from the Small Blind, has raised. Calling would put me out of position with a dominated hand, and there is no reason to bluff here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I am on the button with K6o in a 3-handed game. While K6o is not a premium hand, it is typically strong enough to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of positional play. A standard 2.5x open is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 8h, which is a very weak hand. Calling from out of position against a button raise is generally negative expectation, and there is no reason to inflate the pot with such low-ranking cards. Folding is the disciplined, correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button. My hand, Ten-Five offsuit, is a very weak starting hand that lacks both high card value and playability. Defending this hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser is heavily negative expected value (-EV). The correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with A3s, a hand that has good playability and positional advantage. Since the action is on me, opening with a standard raise is the optimal strategy in a 3-handed game to either win the blinds immediately or secure the initiative for post-flop play.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, Jc 2h. The Button has raised to 2.5x the big blind. Calling or raising with this hand is a losing play in the long run. I will fold.

Queen-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand even from the button in a 3-handed game. While a steal attempt is possible, it is often better to fold this easily dominated offsuit combination and wait for better spots, especially with a comfortable stack of over 100 BBs.

I am on the button with Q2o. This is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While I have the positional advantage, Q2o doesn't play well post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify a raise or a limp. I will fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

Holding J-10 suited on the button is a strong, playable hand that benefits from having position. Opening the pot with a raise allows me to take the initiative, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable post-flop situation.

Holding the lowest suited connector (2h3h) in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button. This hand is too weak to call or 3-bet out of position, as it plays poorly post-flop and rarely makes strong enough hands to justify the investment. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I have been dealt pocket Queens, which is a premium hand. Flashlight opened from the button for 300. I am in position on the button. The board is 9c-7d-6h-Kd. My hand is Jh-Th, giving me a gutshot straight draw (I need an 8 to complete the 6-7-8-9-T straight). Flash has checked the turn. I have completed a straight (7-8-9-10-J) on the river, using the 9-8-7-6 on the board and my Jack-Ten. A bet of 2500 should be large enough to look tempting or induce a crying call from a lower straight (e.g., 5-X) or two-pair/set hands, but not so large that it folds out everything.

The river 8h is a very dangerous card as it completes a four-to-a-straight (6-7-8-9). Any Ten or Five now makes a straight. Additionally, the King on the turn already outranks my pocket Queens. Since I only beat a pure bluff or a lower pair like JJ (which would likely check back for showdown), folding is the most prudent play to preserve my remaining stack.

Nine-Eight offsuit is a marginal and weak hand even from the Button. While stealing the blinds is an option, it's generally more optimal to fold offsuit connectors to avoid tricky post-flop situations when called or 3-bet.

I have been dealt Pocket Jacks (Jc, Jd) in the Small Blind. With the Button (Pro) folding, I am head-up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). I started with Pocket Jacks, which was a strong pre-flop hand, but the flop (Ks, 4c, Ac) is very unfavorable. With middle pair on an Ace-high board, my hand is likely a bluff-catcher or a showdown-value hand. Flash checked to me, which could indicate weakness or a trap. Checking back is the most prudent play, as it controls the pot size and allows me to see a turn card for free. Betting into this board often forces out hands I beat while getting called by hands that have me crushed, so I will take the free card.

I hold a pair of Kings with a Jack kicker on a board of Ks, 4c, Ac, 7d. Flash has checked to me twice, which suggests they might be weak or have a marginal holding. The river is the Queen of Diamonds, which means the board now contains an Ace, King, and Queen. My pocket Jacks have been counterfeited by three overcards. This passivity suggests that Flashlight likely doesn't have a strong Ace or King, but the Queen on the river provides another way for my hand to be beaten. If Flashlight bets, I'll have to evaluate the size, but for now, checking is the most standard play to minimize losses on a very coordinated and high-card board.

With a pair of Kings on an Ace-Queen high board, my showdown value is marginal. Betting would likely only be called by better hands, while checking allows me to realize my showdown value against missed draws or lesser pairs, minimizing any potential loss.

I am on the button with Queen-Five offsuit. While this hand can occasionally be used as a stealing hand, it is quite weak. With Q9o in the small blind and the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. My hand (73o) is extremely weak. Since the Small Blind limped, I can just check and realize any minimal equity I have by seeing a free flop, rather than inflating the pot out of position with a bluff.

I have seven-high with absolutely no draw on an Ace-Jack high board. The small blind has led out with a bet, making this an easy and standard fold.

Pocket 7s is a strong hand against a Button open, but playing it out of position in a multi-way pot can be tricky. 3-betting to 1000 puts pressure on the Button, gives me the initiative, and deters the Big Blind from squeezing or overcalling. The turn brings a 9, which is an unfavorable card for my pocket 7s. I am in position on the turn with Ace-Jack high on a board of 6-5-5-9. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply and potentially catch a bluff from busted high cards.

I hold Ace-Jack high on a board of 6-5-5-9-Q. My opponent (Pro) has checked the river, indicating they are either giving up on the hand with air or are potentially trapping with a made hand. K3o is a weak offsuit king. Even on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call. The best action is to fold.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. With King-high, I have a playable hand in this position. The cost to call is only 50 to see a flop in a 150-pot situation, which is a standard play to see if I can pair my King or find a favorable board.

The hand 7c 9s is a weak, unsuited holding that does not have enough equity to justify calling the 100 chip bet to see a flop. Folding here is the most disciplined and standard strategy to preserve my stack.

With JQs in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I have a very strong hand for a Blind vs Blind encounter. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially win the blinds right now while being perfectly comfortable playing post-flop against the Big Blind if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, particularly in a blind-vs-blind situation. Pro raised from the Small Blind to 300. Since I have position on Pro for the rest of the hand and a premium holding, I will 3-bet to 900. This builds value, takes initiative, and allows me to capitalize on my positional advantage.

I hold Jack-Queen suited, which is a very strong hand in a blind versus blind situation. I have top pair with a good kicker (Jack-Queen) and a gutshot straight draw on a very wet and coordinated board (8d 9d Jc). I have flopped Top Pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board that is quite coordinated with both straight and flush draws available (8d-9d-Jc). A bet of 900, which is half the pot, is a standard and effective sizing in this situation to maintain initiative and build the pot.

We have top pair with a strong kicker (Jack-Queen) and a gutshot straight draw on a very wet board (8d 9d Jc). Calling is the most balanced and profitable approach, allowing us to keep his range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

The King on the turn hits Flash's 3-betting and continuation betting range perfectly, connecting with hands like AK, KQ, and KK. I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit on the button, which is a very weak hand. Given that there is no previous action in this hand and my position, playing this hand would be a mistake. I will fold to conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I have the initiative after raising pre-flop. The flop (Qs, Tc, 5s) contains two high cards that fit well within my perceived raising range. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to apply pressure to Flashlight, who has shown a tendency to fold to post-flop aggression in previous hands. If called, I still have overcards and can reassess on the turn, but I expect to take the pot down here a significant portion of the time.

I hold middle pair (Tens) on a board of 5s-Tc-Qs. The opponent is betting 300 into a 600 pot. This is a standard continuation bet on a somewhat dry board. My pair of Tens has significant showdown value against a wide range, and folding to a single bet would be premature. Raising does not extract much value and would likely only get called by better hands. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card pairs the Queen, which was the top card on the flop. After my flop c-bet was called, I have to consider that Flashlight likely has a piece of this board, such as a Ten, a Five, or a spade draw. While the second Queen is a good card to continue representing a strong range, checking here is the most prudent move for pot control. I have King-high with no significant draw, and since I am the massive chip leader, there is no need to risk more chips in a bloated pot against a range that just called a flop bet. I will check to see a free river or evaluate Flashlight's action.

The board has paired on the turn with the Queen of Diamonds. My pair of Tens is now essentially a bluff catcher. Flash checked, which could indicate a loss of interest in the hand or a trap. Betting here does not serve much value as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would be putting myself in a difficult position if check-raised. Checking back allows me to maintain showdown value and see a cheap river card.

On the river, I have improved to a two-pair (Kings and Queens) with a Ten kicker. The board is now quite coordinated, featuring three spades and potential straights (J9 or AJ). While my hand is strong in a vacuum, a bet here is unlikely to be called by many worse hands, as any Ten or Five will likely fold to significant pressure. Conversely, I am beaten by any Queen, any flush, or a straight. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Flashlight to potentially bluff with missed draws or value bet a weaker hand. Given my chip lead and the texture of the board, checking to see a showdown is the safest and most strategic play.

I am holding a pair of Tens on a board that features a King, Queen, and another Ten. This means my pair of Tens is extremely vulnerable to any hand with a King, Queen, or Ace, which are very likely in the opponent's range given the pre-flop raise and continuation bet. Since the opponent has checked the river, betting my hand would likely only be called by better hands, and checking behind allows me to reach showdown and minimize potential losses. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown cheaply.

I am on the button with 4h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Given my significant chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament (hand 120 of 150), there is no need to play speculative or trash hands. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (2h, 8s). While I have already posted 50 chips, there is no value in completing the blind or raising with such a poor range. It is mathematically better to fold and surrender the 50 chips than to play this hand out of position against the big blind.

I am holding a very weak hand (10d 5h) on the button. There is no incentive to enter the pot with this holding, as it is dominated by most ranges and offers poor equity. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

With 6s 2c offsuit, I have one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the small blind discount, it's not worth playing out of position against the chip leader. Folding is the optimal decision here.
I'm on the Button with a very weak suited hand (62s). With 84.5 big blinds and playing 3-handed, there's no need to open with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I have Ace-Deuce suited in the small blind, which is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since the button folded, I am only up against the big blind. Raising is the standard aggressive play here to either win the pot immediately or take control of the hand with a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 chips.

I am in the big blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with low equity. Flash has raised to 300 from the small blind. Given that I am out of position and the hand strength is extremely poor, calling the 200 chips to see a flop is a negative expected value play. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 98o. In a 3-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I have the chip lead, and a standard 3x open allows me to take control of the hand with position.

Q6 offsuit is a very weak hand, particularly when playing out of position from the Small Blind against a standard opening raise. Defending with such a marginal holding is likely to result in difficult post-flop scenarios and negative expected value over the long term. Folding is the disciplined choice.

With A2s in the Big Blind facing a Button open, we have a hand with good playability and nut flush potential. Calling is a standard and profitable defense against a wide Button opening range. 3-betting is an option, but calling keeps the variance lower and allows us to realize our equity against a wide range.

On a paired A-T-T board out of position, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play. While I have top pair, my kicker is the worst possible, so I want to control the size of the pot and allow my opponent to c-bet his bluffs or check back worse hands. Leading out would generally only get called or raised by better hands.

On this board texture (A-T-T), I have very little equity with 9-high. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage that includes Aces and potentially tens (though less likely given I'm the open-raiser). The big blind checked to me. A continuation bet here is standard to represent the Ace and put pressure on the opponent's weaker holdings or pocket pairs. A small bet of roughly half the pot should be sufficient to fold out hands that missed the flop without risking too many chips if I'm called or raised.

Facing a standard half-pot continuation bet on an A-T-T board, my top pair with a weak kicker is definitely too strong to fold. The opponent's range for c-betting this texture includes many bluffs, underpairs, and floats that I currently beat. However, raising would isolate me against better Aces or Tens, making it an overplay. Calling is the perfect line to keep bluffs and worse hands in the pot while keeping it small.

On the turn, the 8 of spades completes a potential flush draw and adds straight draws, though it mainly serves as a brick against my top pair. My Ace with a 2 kicker is essentially playing the board's 8 as its fifth card, meaning I chop with any Ace that has a 7 or worse kicker, but lose to A9 or better. Checking out of position remains the best play. It keeps the pot manageable, allows my opponent to continue bluffing with drawing hands or air, and saves me money against stronger Aces, Tens, or combinations that improved on the turn.

On the turn, an 8 of spades appears, giving me bottom pair (eights) on a board of A-T-T-8. My opponent, Pro, checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. While the 8 improved my hand to a pair, I still lose to any Ace or Ten, as well as pocket pairs 99 through KK. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and attempt to realize my showdown value or potentially improve on the river. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (Aces or Tens) and folds out hands I'm already beating, so checking is the most prudent play to reach a cheap showdown.

The King on the river is an interesting card because it improves the board's kicker. My hand is now Two Pair (Aces and Tens) with a King kicker, meaning I chop with any opposing Ace from A2 to AQ. I lose to AK, any Ten, or a full house, and I beat Kx, underpairs, and missed draws. Betting doesn't achieve much value since worse hands will likely fold and better hands will call or raise. Checking allows my opponent to bluff with missed draws or mistakenly value-bet a King, while keeping the pot manageable if they happen to have a Ten or AK.

On the river, the board is Ac-Ts-Th-8s-Kc. I have 9s-8c, which gives me a pair of Tens (on the board) with an Ace, King, and Nine kicker. Looking at the action, Pro called my flop bet on a board containing an Ace and two Tens. This suggests Pro has at least an Ace, a Ten, or a pocket pair. The turn check-around was standard. On the river, the King appearing adds another overcard. My 9-kicker is slightly better than the 8 on the board, but I lose to any Ace, King, Ten, or pocket pair higher than Eights (though everyone has at least Tens). Since I am unlikely to fold out a better hand by betting, and I have very little showdown value, checking back to minimize losses is the most logical choice.

Pocket treys are a playable small pair, particularly from the button position. Raising to 300 allows me to potentially take down the blinds immediately or isolate an opponent, maintaining the initiative heading into the flop where I will have position.

Holding 8-4 offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button, I have a very weak hand with extremely low equity. There is no justification to call or 3-bet this trash hand. Folding is the only optimal play to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Flashlight, the button, raised to 300. Pro folded the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450. The pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, meaning I need about 31% equity to make this call profitable. Given that the button opening range is typically wide, Q9o is a standard defending hand in this spot, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand. I will call to see the flop and preserve my chip lead.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on a relatively safe board (Q-7-5 with two hearts). In a blind-versus-button encounter, top pair is a very strong hand. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) to allow them to continuation bet with their wide button opening range, which includes many hands I currently beat. This is a standard play to protect my range and extract value from bluffs or weaker holdings.

I hold a pocket pair of treys on a board of Queen-high with no direct improvement. Since I am the pre-flop aggressor and my opponent checked to me, checking back is the most balanced strategy. Betting offers little value as I will likely only get called by better hands, and checking keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to evaluate the turn card for free.

I have top pair (Queens) with a Nine kicker. On the flop, the action went check-check after I checked to the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight's check on the flop often indicates a hand that lacks high confidence or a marginal holding. The turn Jack of Clubs is relatively safe, though it does bring some straight draws. Since Flashlight showed weakness by checking back on the flop, I will now lead out with a value bet. This aims to get called by middle pairs, pocket pairs, or draws that might have picked up equity. A bet of about 70% of the pot should be effective for extracting value.

The opponent checked the flop and has now bet 450 on the turn board of Qh-7s-5h-Jc. My pocket treys have no showdown value here, as any pair higher than a trey beats me, and the board contains two overcards. There is no logical reason to continue with this hand, as it is an easy fold.
A4s is a strong hand to open from the Button. It plays well post-flop, has nut flush potential, and blocks strong aces. I will raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 8s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. This is a 2.5x open. While my hand has some connectivity, it is weak in a 3-way pot or even heads-up out of position against a player who is showing aggression. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead, as calling puts me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fours. The button has raised to 250, and the small blind has folded. The pot odds (150 to win a pot of 400) are excellent for calling to set-mine. Given my stack size, calling is a low-risk way to proceed, and I avoid overplaying a small pair out of position by 3-betting. I will call the 150 to see the flop.

I flopped a bottom set with 4s-4d on a board of Tc-4h-9c. This is an incredibly strong hand. The board has a potential flush draw and some straight connectors, so I want to build the pot immediately to extract value from overpairs (like JJ or QQ) or top pairs (like AT or KT) and to charge any drawing hands. A bet of 350 into the 550 pot is a solid, standard sizing to accomplish this.

I flopped bottom pair along with the nut flush draw, giving me massive equity against almost any holding Villain might have. While raising is an option to apply pressure, calling in position is very strong here. It keeps Villain's bluffs in the pot, controls the size of the pot, and guarantees I get to see the turn and realize my equity while retaining positional advantage.

I have a set of fours on a board of Tc-4h-9c-3c. This is a very strong hand, but the board is becoming 'wet' with the flush draw possibility. I need to bet to extract value from top pairs (like AT or KT) and straight draws, while also charging any flush draws to continue. Checking allows for a free card which I want to avoid. A bet of 700 into the 1250 pot is a solid sizing to accomplish these goals.

I have turned the absolute nut flush with the Ac 4c. Villain continues to barrel when the flush completes. Since I hold the Ac, Villain cannot be semi-bluffing with the nut flush blocker. They either have a worse flush, a straight, a set, or are bluffing. Raising here might fold out their bluffs and weaker value hands. By flat calling, I keep their range wide, keep them in the hand with their bluffs, and set up a comfortable river SPR (around 1.3) to get the rest of their stack.

The board has completed with a flush draw (three clubs) and possible straight combinations (KJ, J8). My set of fours is strong, but it is vulnerable to these made hands. Betting on the river is risky as I will likely only be called by hands that beat me (a flush or straight), while worse hands will fold. Checking allows me to control the pot and get to showdown. If the opponent bets, I can re-evaluate, but checking is the most prudent play to avoid value-cutting myself.

I hold the nut flush on a board with three clubs. Villain led the flop and turn, then checked the river. The river Qd doesn't change much, except possibly completing a straight like KJ or giving two pair to QT/Q9. Since Villain checked, they likely don't have a strong flush, but might have a marginal made hand like a straight, two pair, or a worse flush that they are pot-controlling. I have the absolute nuts and need to bet for value. A sizing around half to two-thirds pot targets their bluff-catchers, two pairs, and weaker flushes. Betting too large might fold out these marginal hands. I will bet 1500 to extract maximum value.

I have a set of fours on a board that features a potential flush (three clubs) and some straight possibilities. While the opponent's river raise indicates strength, it is quite possible they are overvaluing top pair (like AQ) or two-pair (like Q9, T9, or QT). Given the pot odds of approximately 2.7:1 and the fact that I have a set, folding would be too exploitable and overly cautious. I will call to see if my set holds up against the opponent's range, which likely includes many hands I beat.

I am on the button with Qh-Tc, a strong broadway hand in a 3-handed game. I have a significant chip lead. Standard strategy is to open-raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. Flashlight is short-stacked with only 25BBs, so I must be aware that a raise might induce a shove, but QTo is too strong to fold here.

I am in the small blind with a weak starting hand (Jh 4d). The button has raised, and I am out of position. Calling 250 into a pot where I will be at a disadvantage post-flop is not a sound strategic play. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

K4s is a weak suited king that is generally a fold against a 3x open from the Button, especially when playing out of position. The reverse implied odds and marginal playability postflop make folding the most solid choice here.

My starting hand, 7d 3c, is extremely weak and not worth playing, especially considering my short stack of 2500. As the button, I should conserve my chips and wait for a more advantageous situation to capitalize on a double-up rather than committing capital with a subpar hand.
K4o is a weak hand to play from the small blind against the big blind, especially when out of position against the chip leader. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

I am on the Button with a suited Ace (Ad3d). This is a strong hand to open-raise with to steal the blinds or play in position post-flop. I'll use my standard pre-flop raise sizing of 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Given the hand strength and being out of position, there is no reason to defend or call this raise. Additionally, Flashlight in the Big Blind is short-stacked and could potentially shove. I will preserve my chips and wait for a better spot.

I am defending my big blind with Jack-Nine suited against a button raise. The pot odds are favorable (150 to call into a 400 pot), and this hand has decent post-flop playability even with my short stack.

I am in the Big Blind with a flush draw on an Ace-high board. Checking is the standard play when out of position with a draw, allowing me to see the opponent's action. If they bet, I can evaluate the pot odds for calling or potentially raising if I feel I can take the pot down, but for now, checking is the most strategic approach to keep the pot controlled.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board with flush and straight draws. A continuation bet is necessary to protect my hand, charge draws, and extract value from worse pairs like a Ten or a worse Ace. Given the pot size of 550 and my opponent's remaining stack, a bet of around 200 is well-sized to accomplish this while controlling the size of the pot.

I have a flush draw on an Ace-high board, which provides significant equity in this spot. The opponent's bet of 200 into a 750 pot is very small, offering excellent pot odds to continue. Given my stack size, calling is the most prudent move to see if I can hit my flush or re-evaluate on the turn while keeping my potential to win the pot intact.

I am in the Big Blind with a flush draw on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the button, checking is the standard strategic play to control the pot and see the river card. Given my short stack size, keeping the pot manageable is a priority, and checking allows me to evaluate the opponent's action before committing further chips.

The turn card brings a third spade to the board, completing potential flush draws while simultaneously giving me two pair (Aces and Threes). Since I do not hold a spade for a redraw, my two pair is vulnerable. Checking back allows me to practice pot control, keeps my opponent's bluffs or marginal one-pair hands in the pot, and avoids the risk of being check-raised by a made flush. I can safely evaluate the river to either call a bet or value bet if checked to.

I missed my flush draw and only hold Jack-high. Since my opponent checked back on the turn, they likely have a marginal hand, a weak Ace, or a small pair. Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to fold out their entire range, and my hand has very little showdown value. Therefore, checking is the most strategic play to avoid inflating the pot with a weak hand and to potentially see a showdown for free.

I have rivered a full house, Threes full of Aces, which is an exceptionally strong hand that beats all flushes and straights. The only hands that beat me are pocket Tens, pocket Fives, or the last two Aces, which are highly unlikely given the action. Since my opponent checked the river, they likely hold a marginal made hand like a single Ace or perhaps a hidden flush they are afraid to bet. To extract maximum value from hands like AQ, AJ, or A9, I will make a value bet of around two-thirds of the pot. This sizing is designed to be called by top pair.

I hold a flush (Jack-high) with Js 9s on a board of 5s Th As 3s 3h. Despite the board pairing on the river (the 3h), which raises the possibility of a full house, my flush is an extremely strong hand that cannot be folded. My opponent's river bet is a value bet, and given my hand strength, I must call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with Ten-Six suited. In a three-handed game, this is typically an opening hand. However, Flashlight in the Small Blind is very short-stacked with only 13.5 big blinds. A raise here will often prompt a shove from a short stack looking to double up. T6s is not a hand I want to call a 13BB shove with, and raise-folding would be inefficient. Nevertheless, maintaining aggression on the button is important. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds, but I'm prepared to fold if Flashlight moves all-in, as I want to avoid giving him an easy double-up with a marginal hand.

With a stack of 1350 chips (13.5 BB) at the small blind, my stack is relatively short. Flash has raised from the button, which is a wide opening range. While King-Eight suited is not a premium hand, it is tempting to defend; however, playing out of position against an aggressive button with a short stack is risky. Given the current stack size and the need to pick spots carefully, I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation to double up.

I hold 8h 4d offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, I have virtually no equity or playability to defend my Big Blind. The standard and correct play is to fold and preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

My stack is critically low (1350 chips), which is about 13.5 big blinds. Hand [5d, 6s] is a weak, unsuited connector with poor multi-way potential. Calling 100 chips into a 150 pot with this hand is negative expected value and will further deplete my already diminished stack. I need to remain disciplined and wait for a more favorable hand to either shove or play more aggressively. Folding is the optimal strategic decision here to preserve my remaining chips.

With 64s in the small blind against the big blind who covers me, playing out of position with a marginal suited gapper is generally going to be negative EV. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am dealt J3o on the Button. This is a very weak starting hand, well outside of any standard opening range. Additionally, the Big Blind is short-stacked (12.5 BBs) and could easily shove over a raise. The best and only reasonable play here is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 9d, which is a strong starting hand in a head-up situation against the Big Blind, especially after the Button has folded. Flashlight (Big Blind) is very short-stacked with only 12.5 BBs (1250 chips). Raising to 300 is my standard opening size and puts maximum pressure on the short stack, often winning the pot immediately or setting up a favorable post-flop situation if they call.

My hand (8d, 7s) is extremely weak, and with a short stack of 1250, I need to be selective about the hands I play. Calling a raise from the small blind with such a marginal hand is negative expected value, so I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Q9s on the button, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight is very short-stacked with only 12 BBs, so a raise puts him under significant pressure. If he decides to shove, I will likely have the correct pot odds to call with this hand. Pro is the main stack to worry about, but opening on the button is standard here to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. I will use my standard opening size of 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 12 big blinds (1200 chips). Flash (Button) raised to 300, which is a wide opening move. With 4h-6h suited, I am in an unfavorable position (OOP) against an aggressive opponent. Calling would commit a significant portion of my remaining stack, and 12BBs is too short for speculative play. Shoving is an option, but 64s is borderline and I prefer to wait for a slightly stronger hand to commit my remaining stack.

K4o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button, especially when out of position against the chip leader. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack.

I am in the button position with a very short stack of 12 big blinds. Holding Jack-high with an offsuit kicker (Jh 3c) is extremely weak. There is no strategic value in calling or raising with this hand, as I will likely be dominated by the blinds. It is best to fold, preserve my remaining chips, and wait for a more suitable opportunity to push my stack.
With K8o in the small blind and the button folded, I have a decent high-card hand to attempt a steal against the big blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flash to defend with worse.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine suited (Td 9d), a very strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Additionally, I will have position on Pro for the remainder of the hand. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to realize the equity of my suited connector while keeping the pot manageable against a deep stack. I will call to see the flop.

I flopped top pair on a board that is relatively disconnected but features a flush draw. Betting here protects my hand against those flush and straight draws, extracts value from worse pairs or floats, and maintains the betting lead. A half-pot bet of 300 is an appropriate sizing to charge draws while controlling the pot size given my mediocre kicker.

I have Ten-Nine of diamonds on a board of 4d-Ks-2s. While I have a backdoor diamond flush draw, I have no made hand and no direct straight or flush draws. The King-high board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), who has led out with a half-pot continuation bet. Calling here would be a 'float' with very little equity, and since I am currently the chip leader, there is no reason to risk chips on such a marginal holding. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand, Ten-Six offsuit. The Big Blind has a short stack (11 BBs) and will likely look for spots to shove, reducing my fold equity on a steal attempt. Folding is the standard and optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 2h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and Flashlight (Big Blind). Flashlight is extremely short-stacked with only 1100 chips (11 BB). While I could theoretically complete the small blind for 50 more chips to see a flop, 72s performs poorly even when it hits. Furthermore, raising would likely induce a shove from such a short stack, and I have no desire to call a stack-off with this hand. Given my significant chip lead and the few hands remaining in the tournament, the most disciplined play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

Hand #135: Holding Jack-Deuce offsuit on the button. While it is a three-handed game, J2o is significantly below the standard opening range. Flashlight is short-stacked with only 1200 chips (12 BB) and is highly incentivized to shove over a button raise with any semi-decent holding. I currently hold the chip lead with 15800 and there is no reason to risk chips or provide the short stack with an opportunity to double up with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. With only 12 blinds left in my stack, I need to play cautiously. Q-T offsuit is a reasonable hand to see a flop, and calling the 50 chips completes the Big Blind's bet, allowing me to see the board cheaply.

With pocket Nines against a Small Blind limp, I have a very strong hand. Flashlight is playing a short stack (about 12 BBs). I will raise to 350 to apply pressure and build the pot. This size can induce an all-in from weaker pairs, Ax, or broadway hands, and if he shoves, I am fully prepared to call. If he folds, I happily take down the dead money.

I am in the Small Blind with Q-T offsuit facing a raise from the Big Blind. Given my short stack (1150 chips), folding Q-T to a single raise is too tight, but shoving may be an overreaction. Calling the raise to 350 allows me to see a flop with a decent hand while retaining my stack for future moves. This balances pot odds with risk management.

I have flopped top two pair (Q-T) on a rainbow board (7h-Td-Qh), which is a very strong hand. Given the pot size is 700 and I have only 900 chips remaining, I am pot-committed. Betting all-in allows me to maximize value from hands like weaker pairs, an Ace with a Queen or Ten, or straight draws, while also protecting my hand on this board. It is the most profitable action for my remaining stack.

The flop is very wet with 7h Td Qh, and I only hold pocket Nines, which is third pair to the board. Flashlight limp-called preflop and now open-shoves for more than the size of the pot. His range here includes many hands that beat me (Qx, Tx, strong combo draws) and flush draws against which I am only slightly ahead or flipping. Given that I'm crushed by his value range and only marginally ahead of his draws, folding is the most disciplined and profitable decision. I will conserve my stack for a better spot.

With 6-3 offsuit, I have a very weak hand in a short-stacked situation (1600 chips). Even with the pot odds offered, it is strategically better to fold and wait for a more playable hand than to waste chips on such a low-equity holding.

KQs is a premium starting hand, especially in a blind versus blind situation. With the button folding, raising to 3x the big blind is the standard play to build the pot, extract value from weaker hands in the Big Blind's calling range, and seize the betting initiative going into the flop.

I am holding T9 suited in the Big Blind. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind. This is a very standard spot to defend. T9 suited is a strong speculative hand that flops well. I am getting 2:1 odds on the call (200 more to win a total pot that will be 600). I will call to see the flop and play positionally since I'm the Big Blind against the Small Blind.

I flopped a very strong semi-bluffing hand with a King-high flush draw and two overcards on a paired board. A continuation bet of half the pot is standard to apply pressure, potentially win the pot immediately, and build the pot for when my draw hits. Flash's calling range pre-flop includes many hands that completely missed this board, allowing me to take it down now, and I have great equity even if called.

On a paired board of 7s 7c 5s, I am holding Ten-high with Td 9d. The Small Blind (Pro) has followed up their pre-flop raise with a 300-chip continuation bet into a 600-chip pot.
